origin: Eastern & central North American prairies and open woodlands
habitat: Open prairies, meadows, roadsides and woodland edges on moist to average soils
blurb: The eastern American prairie beardtongue that tolerates clay and moister ground — tall white foxglove-like wands in early summer, glossy foliage (maroon-burgundy on 'Husker Red' and 'Dark Towers'), and persistent seed capsules through winter. More adaptable to Colorado's denser soils than the xeric Western penstemons, and just as attractive to bees and hummingbirds.
sun: Full sun to part shade (at least 4–6 hours)
water: Low to moderate; more moisture- and clay-tolerant than xeric Western penstemons — needs supplemental water at lower Front Range elevations
seasons: White foxglove-like wands in late spring–early summer; glossy green (or maroon on cultivars) foliage through growing season; dark oval seed capsules persist on stems into winter
wildlife: Excellent native-bee and bumblebee plant; specialist bee Osmia distincta uses it; visited by hummingbirds and butterflies

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zones 3a–8b — one of the most cold-hardy penstemons and reliably perennial across the Front Range (Boulder is zone 5b–6a).[1][2] Unlike the xeric Western beardtongues, this eastern prairie species comes from moister habitats and is noticeably more tolerant of average garden soil and heavier clay, though it still demands good drainage to avoid crown rot.[2]
care.planting: Set out container plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall (August–September) so roots establish before winter. Unlike the xeric Western penstemons, P. digitalis accepts average amended border soil and even clay that drains reasonably well — it does not insist on lean, gritty conditions.[1][2] In pots use a loamy, well-drained mix; container roots are far less cold-hardy than ground roots, so move pots to an unheated garage or sink them into the ground over winter.[1]
care.sun: Full sun (6+ hours) produces the most upright stems and best bloom; tolerates partial shade (4–6 hours), which can help in hot Front Range afternoons.[1][2] In too much shade stems flop and bloom declines, but this species handles part shade better than the xeric Western penstemons.
care.soil: Adapts well to clay, loam or sandy soils as long as drainage is adequate — far more soil-flexible than the xeric Western beardtongues.[1][2] Boulder's heavy alkaline clay is tolerated if the planting area does not pool water; no special grit amendment is needed. NC State reports soil pH preference of acidic (<6.0), but Front Range soils are typically 7–8 and this species is grown successfully here — avoid heavily fertilized, wet soils rather than worrying about pH.[2]
care.water: Low to moderate; significantly more moisture-tolerant than xeric Western penstemons and will need supplemental irrigation at lower Front Range elevations — unlike P. strictus, it will not thrive on rainfall alone in a dry year.[1] A deep soak every 1–2 weeks through the hottest summer stretches is appropriate once established; reduce after flowering to avoid crown rot, but don't let it dry out completely.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants 18–24 in apart; mature clumps reach 18–24 in wide and 2–3 ft tall in flower.[1][2] Set in drifts of three or more for the most impact; the dark-foliage cultivars read best massed against lighter-colored companions.
care.propagation: From seed: cold-moist stratify 4–6 weeks at 34–40°F (or fall-sow outdoors for natural winter chilling) before a late-winter indoor start.[1] Sow shallowly — seed needs light to germinate; surface-sow or cover only 1/16 in. Plants typically first bloom in their second year. Division: unlike many penstemons, P. digitalis clumps can be divided in spring or early fall — lift and separate into sections each with roots and a growing point, replant at the same depth.[2] The dark-foliage cultivars ('Husker Red', 'Dark Towers') must be propagated by division or basal stem cuttings (taken in early summer) to come true to type; seed-grown plants revert to green foliage.[2]
care.sow: Surface-sow or barely cover (1/16 in) into a firm seedbed; seed needs light for good germination. Fall outdoor sowing supplies natural cold stratification.[1]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify 4–6 weeks at ~38°F to break dormancy, or fall-sow outdoors for natural winter chilling.[1]
care.bloom: White, 1-in. foxglove-like tubular flowers in open branching panicles from late spring into early summer (May–July on the Front Range).[1][2] The 2-lipped flowers have a distinctly swollen middle and purple lines inside the throat. Deadhead spent stalks to tidy the plant; leaving a few stalks allows self-sowing and preserves the dark oval seed capsules, which persist and provide winter structure.[1][2]
care.feeding: Little or no fertilizer needed in average garden soil.[1] A light spring topdressing of compost is ample; avoid rich feeding, which produces lanky growth and weakens the plant.
care.maintenance: Low. Remove spent flower stalks unless you want self-sowing or the attractive seed capsule display.[1][2] Basal foliage is semi-evergreen; the dark-leaved cultivars hold their burgundy color through winter. Divide clumps every 3–4 years when the center begins to die out — this species divides more readily than most penstemons.[2]
care.troubles: No serious disease or pest problems reported.[2] Crown and root rot can develop in wet, waterlogged soil; ensure good drainage. In our dry climate the main failure mode is underwatering, not overwatering — unlike the xeric species. Occasional grasshoppers and rust fungi.[1]
care.companions: A natural fit for native pollinator gardens paired with other prairie forbs: prairie coneflower, black-eyed Susan, blanket flower, wild bergamot, little bluestem, and switchgrass. Its white flower wands and dark-foliage cultivars contrast beautifully with yellow or orange daisy-flowers and blue-violet native sage. Taller than most native penstemons, so it works at mid-border or back-of-border.[2]

references.name: NC State Extension Plant Toolbox — Penstemon digitalis

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally as a native-bee and hummingbird pollinator plant — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: Despite being called 'foxglove beardtongue,' Penstemon digitalis is NOT related to foxglove (Digitalis) and does not carry foxglove's cardiac-glycoside toxicity.[2] It has no known culinary use and is not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it. Don't let the common name suggest otherwise.
